content:
{
  "title": "Adaptive Contrastive Prompting for Robust Defense Against Adversarial Jailbreaks",
  "problem": "Current prompt-based defenses often fail against sophisticated adversarial prompts and jailbreak attempts that exploit language model vulnerabilities. There is a need for prompting methods that dynamically adapt to and counteract adversarial intent.",
  "motivation": "As language models grow more powerful and accessible, adversarial attacks and jailbreaks pose increasing real-world risks. Existing static or rule-based prompting approaches can be circumvented by creative attackers, highlighting the need for more robust, context-aware defenses that can generalize to unforeseen attacks.",
  "method": "We propose Adaptive Contrastive Prompting (ACP), a method where the input prompt is automatically augmented with contrastive negative examples—phrases that rephrase or subtly inject adversarial intent related to the original user input. The model is conditioned to distinguish between benign and adversarial rephrasings within the same context, enabled by in-context demonstrations. ACP leverages real-time prompt analysis to generate hard negative examples and incorporates a meta-prompt that instructs the model to output responses only if the input is dissimilar to adversarial cases. This adaptive, example-driven prompting allows the model to self-check and resist novel jailbreak attempts.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate ACP on standard adversarial prompt datasets (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench) and construct new dynamic attack sets with paraphrased and zero-shot jailbreaks. Baselines include vanilla LLM prompting, static adversarial example prompting, and rule-based defenses. Metrics include attack success rate, response harmlessness (via automated and human evaluation), and task utility to ensure alignment without sacrificing performance."
}
